title: NIS2 OT Incident Detection — Process and Protocol Anomalies

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(f) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(f) (Policies and procedures effectiveness) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.39: NIS2 OT Incident Detection — Process and Protocol Anomalies.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.39' running on index=ot_protocol (HEC from Edge Hub or passive taps), nis2_ot_baseline.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=ot_network` `sourcetype=edge_hub:modbus` (Splunk Edge Hub collectors) carries `function_code`, `register_span`, `unit_id`, `plc_name`, `cell_line`. Companion `sourcetype=edge_hub:opcua` feeds `node_id`, `browse_depth` when OPC-UA pilots exist. Baselines stored in `nis2_ot_baseline.csv` with `expected_fn_variety`, `expected_span`. Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) routes notables to OT SOC queues; Splunk OT Security Add-on (Splunkbase 5151) optional for asset context. Ingest uses HTTP Event Collector token on Heavy Forwarder per **Settings > Data inputs > HTTP Event Collector**.
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk OT Security Add-on (Splunkbase 5151), HTTP Event Collector (HEC — Edge Hub forwarder), Splunk Edge Hub solution documentation
spl:
index=ot_network sourcetype=edge_hub:modbus earliest=-7d
| stats count dc(function_code) as fn_variety avg(register_span) as avg_span by plc_name cell_line
| lookup nis2_ot_baseline.csv cell_line OUTPUT expected_fn_variety expected_span
| eval anomaly=if(fn_variety > expected_fn_variety*2 OR avg_span > expected_span*3, 1, 0)
| where anomaly=1
| table plc_name, cell_line, fn_variety, expected_fn_variety, avg_span, expected_span, anomaly
description: Statistically compares Modbus traffic diversity and register span per PLC/cell to engineering-approved baselines so unusual industrial conversations—possible manipulation or spoofing—surface before they cascade into physical harm.
value: Gives safety and plant leadership audit-grade proof that operational technology is watched with the same seriousness as office systems, by storing baseline deviations with timestamps suitable for regulator briefings after near-misses.
implementation: (1) Capture baselines during golden runs with operations sign-off. (2) Land Edge Hub firehose on `index=ot_network`, never on security-only indexes. (3) Tune multipliers (`*2`, `*3`) with plant engineers. (4) Page OT SOC via ES (Splunkbase 263) with response SLAs. (5) Feed confirmed anomalies into incident ticketing (`snow:incident` via Splunkbase 1928 if adopted).
visualization: Line chart of `fn_variety`, table of anomalous cells, single value anomaly count, heatmap by shift.
knownFalsePositives:
• **Planned recipe experiments** — Distinguish: operations widen `function_code` mix for QA lots. Suppress: lookup `nis2_ot_change_window.csv` keyed on `cell_line` with `experiment=true` and `valid_until`.
• **Firmware downloads** — Distinguish: short spikes when vendor updates PLC. Suppress: time-bound exception register rows referencing `change_ticket`.
• **Simulator labs sharing production names** — Distinguish: lab `plc_name` collides with live tag. Suppress: append `| where NOT match(plc_name,"LAB$")` or segregate lab traffic to `index=ot_lab`.
• **Baseline CSV drift** — Distinguish: baseline stale after throughput upgrade. Suppress: enforce quarterly regeneration workflow; temporary widen multipliers via macro argument.
• **Passive tap packet loss** — Distinguish: span looks huge because mirrored span incomplete. Suppress: correlate with `interface_errors` from tap switch using side join before alerting.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Edge Hub deployed on the OT DMZ or plant-floor network with Modbus TCP collector configured. The Edge Hub must be able to reach the Modbus-speaking PLCs on TCP/502 (Modbus TCP default) or via serial-to-Ethernet converters for Modbus RTU. Confirm the Edge Hub is collecting data via the Edge Hub management UI > Sources > Modbus > Status.
• Heavy Forwarder on the plant DMZ receiving Edge Hub data via HTTP Event Collector (HEC). The HF must have a dedicated HEC token scoped to `index=ot_network` with `sourcetype=edge_hub:modbus`. Never share OT HEC tokens with IT data streams — OT traffic must be isolated for both security and compliance reasons.
• Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) ≥7.0 with a dedicated OT SOC queue configured via Configure > Incident Management > Notable Event Statuses. The OT queue must be separate from the IT SOC queue because OT incident response follows different runbooks (e.g., you cannot remotely isolate a PLC running a live process without safety review).
• Splunk OT Security Add-on (Splunkbase 5151) optional but recommended for asset context enrichment — it maps `plc_name` to plant, line, criticality tier, and safety-instrumented-system (SIS) association.
• CSV `nis2_ot_baseline.csv` with columns: `cell_line` (unique key, e.g., `BOTTLING-L3`, `COMPRESSOR-A`), `expected_fn_variety` (integer — the number of distinct Modbus function codes normally observed during a 7-day window, e.g., 3 for a simple read-only polling pattern), `expected_span` (integer — the normal average register span per transaction, e.g., 40 registers for a typical PLC poll cycle), `baseline_date` (ISO 8601 — when the baseline was last validated by operations engineering), `plant_site` (for multi-site filtering). This baseline must be generated from a "golden run" — a 7-day window where operations confirms the process was running normally with no maintenance, experiments, or anomalies.
• NTP synchronisation: the Edge Hub, Heavy Forwarder, and PLC engineering workstations must be synchronised within 100ms of the plant NTP source. Modbus itself has no timestamps — the Edge Hub applies `_time` at collection. Clock drift >1 second causes the 7-day lookback to capture partial data and skew `avg_span` calculations.
• RBAC: create role `ot_analyst` with `srchIndexesAllowed = ot_network, audit_evidence`. OT data is sensitive — it can reveal production rates, batch compositions, and process parameters that are trade secrets. Do not grant IT SOC analysts access unless dual-role.
• License: Modbus polling at 1-second intervals per PLC produces ~86,400 events/PLC/day at ~200 bytes/event ≈ 17 MB/PLC/day. A 50-PLC plant ingests ~850 MB/day. At 400-day retention, plan ~340 GB for `index=ot_network`.
• Baseline knowledge: Modbus function codes — FC1 (Read Coils), FC2 (Read Discrete Inputs), FC3 (Read Holding Registers), FC4 (Read Input Registers), FC5 (Write Single Coil), FC6 (Write Single Register), FC15 (Write Multiple Coils), FC16 (Write Multiple Registers). Normal SCADA polling uses FC3/FC4 only. Appearance of FC5/FC6/FC15/FC16 outside maintenance windows is a strong anomaly signal — it means something is writing to PLC registers.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
On the Edge Hub management UI, configure a Modbus TCP source:

• **Connection**: `tcp://<PLC-IP>:502` (one source per PLC or per Modbus gateway if using serial-to-TCP converters).
• **Polling interval**: 1000ms (1 second) for critical PLCs, 5000ms for non-critical. The Edge Hub polls each configured register range at this interval and produces one event per poll response.
• **Register configuration**: define the register ranges to poll in the Edge Hub source config (e.g., Holding Registers 0-99, Input Registers 0-49). The Edge Hub extracts `function_code`, `register_address`, `register_count` (which becomes `register_span`), `unit_id`, and the register values.
• **HEC destination**: configure the Edge Hub to forward to the Heavy Forwarder's HEC endpoint (`https://<HF-IP>:8088/services/collector/event`) with the dedicated OT token. Use TLS — even on the plant network, because Modbus traffic contains process data.

Field extraction verification: after the Edge Hub has been running for at least 5 minutes, run:

```spl
index=ot_network sourcetype=edge_hub:modbus earliest=-10m
| stats dc(function_code) as fn_codes, dc(unit_id) as plc_count, avg(register_span) as avg_span
| table fn_codes, plc_count, avg_span
```

`plc_count` should match the number of PLCs configured in the Edge Hub. `fn_codes` should be 1-4 for normal read-only polling (FC3 and/or FC4). If `fn_codes` is 0 or `plc_count` is 0, the Edge Hub is not successfully polling — check the Edge Hub logs (Management UI > Diagnostics > Logs) for Modbus connection errors (common: PLC IP unreachable, unit_id mismatch, firewall blocking TCP/502).

Field mapping: the Edge Hub produces events with fields `function_code` (integer), `register_span` (integer — number of registers read/written per transaction), `unit_id` (Modbus slave address, 1-247), and `plc_name` (derived from the source name configured in Edge Hub). If `plc_name` is not set, create a field alias in `props.conf`:

```ini
[edge_hub:modbus]
FIELDALIAS-plc_name = source_name AS plc_name
EVAL-cell_line = case(match(plc_name, "^BOTTLING"), "BOTTLING-L3", match(plc_name, "^COMP"), "COMPRESSOR-A", 1==1, "UNKNOWN")
```

The `cell_line` EVAL maps PLC names to the production-line identifiers used in the baseline CSV. This mapping must be maintained by the OT engineering team — it changes when PLCs are reassigned between lines.

Expected event volume: 1 event per PLC per poll interval per register range. A PLC polled at 1-second intervals across 3 register ranges produces 3 events/second ≈ 259,200 events/day. At 200 bytes/event, that is ~52 MB/PLC/day. Scale accordingly.

**Generating the baseline CSV**: run the following during a confirmed normal-operations week (no maintenance, no experiments, no anomalies — operations engineering must sign off):

```spl
index=ot_network sourcetype=edge_hub:modbus earliest=-7d@d latest=@d
| stats dc(function_code) as expected_fn_variety avg(register_span) as expected_span by cell_line
| eval baseline_date=strftime(now(), "%Y-%m-%d")
| outputlookup nis2_ot_baseline.csv
```

Store the output CSV in `etc/apps/SA-NIS2/lookups/` and push via Deployment Server. The baseline must be regenerated quarterly or after any significant process change (new recipe, line reconfiguration, PLC firmware upgrade).

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Save as `UC-22.2.39 NIS2 OT Anomaly Detection`.

```spl
index=ot_network sourcetype=edge_hub:modbus earliest=-7d
| stats count dc(function_code) as fn_variety avg(register_span) as avg_span by plc_name cell_line
| lookup nis2_ot_baseline.csv cell_line OUTPUT expected_fn_variety expected_span
| eval anomaly=if(fn_variety > expected_fn_variety*2 OR avg_span > expected_span*3, 1, 0)
| where anomaly=1
| table plc_name, cell_line, fn_variety, expected_fn_variety, avg_span, expected_span, anomaly
```

Understanding this SPL — design decisions that survive review:

• **7-day rolling window**: Modbus traffic is highly periodic — a 7-day window smooths out daily and weekly maintenance patterns. Shorter windows (1 day) produce more false positives because weekend maintenance shows as anomalous on a weekday baseline.

• **`dc(function_code)` as `fn_variety`**: the distinct count of function codes is the primary anomaly indicator. Normal SCADA polling uses 1-2 function codes (FC3 Read Holding Registers, FC4 Read Input Registers). If `fn_variety` jumps from 2 to 6, it means write operations (FC5, FC6, FC15, FC16) appeared — which could indicate legitimate maintenance, or unauthorised PLC reprogramming (MITRE ICS T0855 — Unauthorised Command Message).

• **`avg(register_span)` as `avg_span`**: the average number of registers read/written per transaction. Normal polling reads fixed register ranges. A sudden increase in span means something is reading or writing larger blocks — possibly a firmware download, a configuration change, or reconnaissance scanning the full register map.

• **Multiplier thresholds (`*2` and `*3`)**: these are deliberately loose to minimise false positives in industrial environments where false alarms have real cost (an operator responding to a spurious alert may shut down a production line). The `*2` multiplier on `fn_variety` means the alarm fires only when function-code diversity doubles — a strong signal. The `*3` multiplier on `avg_span` allows for normal variation in register reads during shift changes. Tighten these only after 90 days of baseline data with plant engineering approval.

• **No time-of-day filtering**: unlike IT security where attack detection can narrow to off-hours, OT attacks can happen at any time — including during shift changes when operators are distracted. Do not add time filters.

Schedule: cron `0 */4 * * *` (every 4 hours). Time range: `-7d to now`. Trigger: `Number of results > 0`. Throttle by `cell_line` for 12 hours — a sustained anomaly on one line should not re-page every 4 hours during investigation.

Alert action: (1) Create an ES notable with `urgency=high`, routed to the OT SOC queue. (2) Page the OT incident responder via PagerDuty with `severity=warning`. Do NOT auto-remediate — in OT, automated responses can cause physical harm. Every response requires human decision. (3) Write results to `index=audit_evidence sourcetype=evidence:saved_search`.

OPC-UA variant: for sites using OPC-UA instead of Modbus, duplicate the search with `sourcetype=edge_hub:opcua` and swap the stats to `dc(node_id) as node_variety, avg(browse_depth) as avg_depth`. The anomaly logic is similar: unexpected node_id diversity suggests reconnaissance of the OPC-UA address space.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Baseline accuracy**: compare the baseline CSV values to the current live statistics. Run `index=ot_network sourcetype=edge_hub:modbus earliest=-7d | stats dc(function_code) as live_fn avg(register_span) as live_span by cell_line | lookup nis2_ot_baseline.csv cell_line OUTPUT expected_fn_variety expected_span | eval fn_delta=abs(live_fn - expected_fn_variety), span_delta=abs(live_span - expected_span)`. If `fn_delta > 0` or `span_delta > expected_span*0.5`, the baseline is stale and needs regeneration.

(b) **Positive test — function code injection**: during an approved maintenance window, use a Modbus testing tool (e.g., ModbusPal, QModMaster, or `pymodbus` Python library) to send FC6 (Write Single Register) commands to a test PLC on a non-production line. Wait for the next search run (or trigger manually). Confirm the cell_line appears in the output with `fn_variety` increased and `anomaly=1`.

(c) **Positive test — register span anomaly**: use a Modbus scanner tool to read a large register range (e.g., registers 0-999 instead of the normal 0-49). Confirm `avg_span` increases and the anomaly triggers.

(d) **Negative test — normal operations**: during confirmed normal operations (no maintenance, no experiments), run the search. It should return 0 rows. If it returns rows, the baseline is too tight or has drifted — regenerate with operations sign-off.

(e) **Edge Hub health**: confirm the Edge Hub is successfully polling all configured PLCs: check the Edge Hub Management UI > Sources > Modbus > each source should show "Connected" status and a recent "Last Data" timestamp. In Splunk: `index=ot_network sourcetype=edge_hub:modbus | stats max(_time) as last_seen by plc_name | eval lag_min=round((now()-last_seen)/60,1) | where lag_min > 10`. Any PLC with `lag_min > 10` is not sending data — check Edge Hub connectivity to that PLC.

(f) **Field extraction sanity**: `index=ot_network sourcetype=edge_hub:modbus earliest=-1h | stats count by function_code | sort function_code`. Expected: FC3 and/or FC4 dominating (>95% of events). FC5/FC6/FC15/FC16 should be <1% during normal operations. If write FCs are consistently present, either the baseline is wrong (operations normally writes to PLCs as part of the process) or there is a standing issue.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard (recommended name: `NIS2 OT Protocol Anomaly Monitor`):
• **Row 1 — Headline tiles**: Single-value `Cell lines with active anomalies` (red >= 1, green = 0). Single-value `PLCs reporting data` (compare to expected count — amber if < 90%). Single-value `Baseline age (days)` from `max(baseline_date)` in the CSV (amber > 60 days, red > 90 days).
• **Row 2 — Function code distribution**: stacked bar chart of `function_code` by `cell_line` over the last 7 days. Write function codes (FC5/6/15/16) should be visually distinct (red) against read codes (FC3/4, green). This gives the OT engineer an immediate visual of "where is writing happening?"
• **Row 3 — Anomaly table**: sortable by cell_line, plc_name, fn_variety, avg_span, with traffic-light indicators (green/amber/red based on proximity to threshold). Drilldown opens a timeline of `function_code` events for that PLC over the last 24 hours.
• **Row 4 — 30-day trend**: timechart of `fn_variety` by `cell_line` over 30 days from `index=audit_evidence`. Shows whether anomaly frequency is increasing (drift) or was a one-time event (maintenance).
• **Row 5 — Shift heatmap**: heatmap of anomaly count by hour-of-day and day-of-week. Clusters around shift changes suggest operator error; clusters at 03:00 AM suggest deliberate activity.

Runbook (owner: OT Incident Responder):
1. PagerDuty alert received. Open the dashboard. Identify the cell_line and plc_name.
2. **DO NOT remotely power-cycle or isolate the PLC without safety review.** OT incident response rule #1: human safety first. If the PLC is controlling a safety-critical process (SIS interlock, pressure relief, emergency shutdown), escalate to the plant safety engineer BEFORE any response action.
3. Check the function-code distribution panel. If write FCs (FC5/6/15/16) appeared: (a) Was there a scheduled maintenance window? Check `index=itsm sourcetype=snow:change_request cmdb_ci=<plc_name> earliest=-24h`. If yes, this is a false positive — close the notable with annotation. (b) If no maintenance window: escalate to OT security lead. This may be an unauthorised PLC modification attempt.
4. Check the register-span panel. If span increased: (a) Was a firmware download in progress? Check with the vendor engineering tool. (b) If no known activity: the span increase may indicate Modbus register scanning — an attacker mapping the PLC's address space before manipulation.
5. After investigation, annotate the ES notable with findings. If confirmed anomaly, open an OT security incident in ServiceNow and preserve the Splunk search results as evidence.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **No events in `index=ot_network`.** The Edge Hub is not sending data to HEC. Check the Edge Hub Management UI > Destinations > HEC status. Common causes: (a) HEC token was disabled after a Splunk restart — re-enable via Settings > Data Inputs > HTTP Event Collector. (b) TLS certificate expired on the Heavy Forwarder — the Edge Hub rejects self-signed certs by default. (c) Network ACL between the Edge Hub and HF was changed — the OT VLAN may have been re-segmented during a network change. Verify with `curl -k https://<HF-IP>:8088/services/collector/health` from the Edge Hub network.

• **`function_code` field is null on all events.** The Edge Hub source is configured for raw Modbus passthrough instead of parsed mode. In raw mode, the Edge Hub sends the hex payload without field extraction. Reconfigure the Edge Hub Modbus source to "Parsed" mode, which extracts `function_code`, `register_address`, `register_span`, and `unit_id` automatically. Alternatively, write a `props.conf` extraction: `EXTRACT-modbus_fc = (?i)function.?code[=:]\s*(?P<function_code>\d+)`.

• **`register_span` is always 0 or null.** The Edge Hub version is <2.0 and does not extract register span. Upgrade the Edge Hub firmware, or compute span from raw fields: `| eval register_span = register_count` if the Edge Hub uses `register_count` instead.

• **Anomaly fires every Monday at 06:00.** Weekly maintenance window where engineers download ladder logic changes. This is a known false positive. Fix: create a `nis2_ot_change_window.csv` lookup with `cell_line`, `day_of_week`, `hour_start`, `hour_end`, and filter: `| lookup nis2_ot_change_window.csv cell_line OUTPUT day_of_week hour_start hour_end | where NOT (date_wday=day_of_week AND date_hour >= hour_start AND date_hour <= hour_end)`. Or add a time-bound exception to the register.

• **Baseline CSV shows `expected_fn_variety=0` for some lines.** The baseline was generated during a planned shutdown when no traffic was flowing. A zero baseline causes the `*2` multiplier to produce 0, meaning ANY traffic triggers an anomaly. Fix: regenerate the baseline during normal operations. As a stopgap, set `expected_fn_variety=1` as a minimum floor.

• **Edge Hub reports "Connection refused" for some PLCs.** The PLC's Modbus TCP server accepts only one concurrent connection — and the SCADA/DCS system is already using it. Most PLCs support only 1-4 concurrent Modbus TCP connections. Fix: use a Modbus gateway/proxy that multiplexes connections, or switch to passive monitoring using a network tap and Zeek with the Modbus parser instead of active polling.

• **Duplicate events from redundant Edge Hubs.** Two Edge Hubs (primary and standby) are both polling the same PLCs, doubling the event count and inflating `fn_variety`. Fix: add an `edge_hub_id` field to the HEC payload via the Edge Hub configuration, and filter to the primary in the search: `| where edge_hub_id="primary"`. Or deduplicate: `| dedup _time unit_id function_code register_address`.

• **`cell_line` is "UNKNOWN" for many PLCs.** The `EVAL-cell_line` mapping in `props.conf` does not have entries for recently added PLCs. Fix: update the case statement in `props.conf` to include the new PLC name patterns. Long-term: maintain the mapping in a lookup instead of `props.conf` — `| lookup ot_plc_to_line.csv plc_name OUTPUT cell_line` — so it can be updated without a Splunk restart.
grandmaExplanation: We learn what "normal" chatter looks like on the factory floor networks, then we speak up when a line starts talking in an unusual way that could mean trouble—not just when office computers misbehave.
dataModelAcceleration: Not required for this use case.
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel/competent-authority review.
evidence: `UC-22.2.39` results listing `plc_name`, `cell_line`, `anomaly`, plus Edge Hub sample payloads and ES notable IDs archived under `audit_evidence` for OT effectiveness reviews.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Lower `expected_fn_variety` in `nis2_ot_baseline.csv` for lab `cell_line=LINE-QA` so the live stats exceed 2× baseline; the search must return `anomaly=1` for that PLC/cell pair.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Restore baseline numbers or halt the injected traffic pattern and rerun over `-7d`; expect the QA line to disappear from the anomaly table when both ratios fall below thresholds.

equipmentModels: Splunk Edge Hub (Modbus TCP/RTU data collection gateway)
Modbus TCP/RTU PLCs (Allen-Bradley, Siemens S7, Schneider Modicon)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (notable framework, OT SOC queues)
Splunk OT Security Add-on (asset context and enrichment macros)
